question_match_notes: Text-only one-variable linear equation. Live instructional sources were opened to support the standard distribute-then-isolate method.

artifact_quality.notes: Text-only academic question with no OCR ambiguity.

answer_scope.scope_notes: Single linear equation solved as one unit.

answer_claims.text: Distribute first: 3(x + 1) = 15 becomes 3x + 3 = 15. Subtract 3 to get 3x = 12, then divide by 3 to get x = 4.

reasoning_steps: Subtract 3 from both sides to get 3x = 12.
Divide both sides by 3 to get x = 4.

controller_supporting_sources.title: 2.2 Linear Equations in One Variable | OpenStax College Algebra 2e
controller_supporting_sources.copied_text: Apply the distributive property as needed and isolate the variable on one side of the equation.
controller_supporting_sources.locator: How To: Given a linear equation in one variable, use algebra to solve it
controller_supporting_sources.why_this_source_matters: Supports distributing first and then isolating x.

controller_supporting_sources.title: 2.1 Linear Equations | Mathematics LibreTexts
controller_supporting_sources.copied_text: Notice the first step is distributing, then it is solved like any other two-step equation.
controller_supporting_sources.locator: Example PageIndex 21 and general strategy for solving linear equations
controller_supporting_sources.why_this_source_matters: Matches the distribute, simplify, and isolate sequence used for this equation.

visible_tutor_cycle.message_to_tutor_now: Distribute the 3 first. What does 3(x + 1) become?
visible_tutor_cycle.if_on_track: Right. Now the equation is 3x + 3 = 15. What should you do next to isolate 3x?
visible_tutor_cycle.if_confused: Use the distributive property: multiply 3 by x and by 1, so 3(x + 1) becomes 3x + 3.
visible_tutor_cycle.silence_breaker: What is the result of distributing 3 over x + 1?
